article:
Chunk # 0.
Isolation of Rat Adrenocortical Mitochondria
Paola Solinas1, Hisashi Fujioka2, Bernard Tandler3, and Charles L Hoppel1,4
1Departments of Pharmacology and of Medicine and Center for Mitochondrial Disease, School of
Medicine, Case Western Reserve University, Cleveland, Ohio 44106
2Electron Microscopy Facility, Department of Pharmacology, and Center for Mitochondrial
Disease, School of Medicine, Case Western Reserve University, Cleveland, Ohio 44106
3Department of Biological Sciences, School of Dental Medicine, and Center for Mitochondrial
Disease, School of Medicine, Case Western Reserve University, Cleveland, Ohio 44106
Abstract
This report describes a relatively simple and reliable method for isolating adrenocortical
mitochondria from rats in good, reasonably pure yield These organelles, which heretofore have
been unobtainable in isolated form from small laboratory animals, are now readily accessible A
high degree of mitochondrial purity is shown by the electron micrographs, as well as the structural
integrity of each mitochondrion That these organelles have retained their functional integrity is
shown by their high respiratory control ratios In general, the biochemical performance of these
adrenal cortical mitochondria closely mirrors that of typical hepatic or cardiac mitochondria Keywords
Adrenal Cortex; Mitochondria; Isolation; Oxidative Phosphorylation; Rat
Introduction
Most biochemical data on adrenocortical mitochondria are based on organelles derived from
domesticated animals, such as bovines [1-4], pigs [5], and sheep [6,7] The use of these large
animals almost always precludes endocrinological experimentation because of the required
© 2012 Published by Elsevier Inc 4Correspondence to: Charles L Hoppel M.D., Case Western Reserve University, School of Medicine, Department of Pharmacology,
10900 Euclid Avenue, Cleveland, OH 44106, Ph: (216) 368-3147, Fax: (216) 368-5162, charles.hoppel@case.edu 1Dr Solinas’s present address is: Department of Cytomorphology, School of Medicine, University of Cagliari, I-09042 Cagliari, Italy
Publisher's Disclaimer: This is a PDF file of an unedited manuscript that has been accepted for publication As a service to our
customers we are providing this early version of the manuscript The manuscript will undergo copyediting, typesetting, and review of
the resulting proof before it is published in its final citable form Please note that during the production process errors may be
discovered which could affect the content, and all legal disclaimers that apply to the journal pertain The authors declare no conflict of interest in the production of this work or in its publication The financial sponsors of this work had
no influence on the content of this study nor on its publication Author Contributions
Paola Solinas participated in the design and carried out the biochemical aspects of the experiment and was involved in the writing;
Hisashi Fujioka performed the electron microscopy and participated in the writing; Bernard Tandler interpreted the electron
micrographs and performed a major share of the writing; Charles L Hoppel participated in the design of the experiment, interpreted
the biochemical data, and was heavily involved in the writing NIH Public Access
Author Manuscript
Biochem Biophys Res Commun  Author manuscript; available in PMC 2014 August 04 Published in final edited form as:
Biochem Biophys Res Commun  2012 October 12; 427(1): 96–99 doi:10.1016/j.bbrc.2012.09.014 NIH-PA Author Manuscript NIH-PA Author Manuscript NIH-PA Author Manuscriptvolume of reactive agents and concomitant expenses involved in maintenance and clinical
observation of such animals Because of their small size, it would be desirable to use rats as
a source of adrenocortical mitochondria, but isolation of these organelles from the rat
adrenal cortex has proven an elusive goal due to the diminutive volume of the adrenal cortex
in these animals As a result, most biochemical studies of rat adrenocortical mitochondria
have been based on tissue slices [8] or cultured cells [9], or cultured mouse adrenal tumor
cells [10] Although much has been learned from these preparations, the presence of in situ
confounding factors in the former cannot easily be gainsaid This report describes a
relatively simple and reliable method for isolating rat adrenocortical mitochondria in good,
reasonably pure yield This method also has applicability to sundry, very small tissue
specimens from which it is desired to isolate mitochondria Materials and Methods
Animal studies were approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committees of
Case Western Reserve University School of Medicine Fischer 344 rats (6 month-old male)
from the NIA colony at Harlan were used They were housed in a temperature- and humidity
controlled animal facility and had free access to food and water On the day of the
experiments, the rats were weighed (weight = 399 ± 21 g; mean ± SD; N=5) and killed by
decapitation between 8 and 9 AM After exsanguination, their adrenal glands were quickly
dissected free of surrounding adipose tissue and excised Isolation of adrenocortical mitochondria
Each individual experiment made use of the paired adrenal glands from a single rat

mitochondria.protocol:
1. "This report describes a relatively simple and reliable method for isolating adrenocortical mitochondria from rats in good, reasonably pure yield."
2. "A high degree of mitochondrial purity is shown by the electron micrographs, as well as the structural integrity of each mitochondrion."
3. "That these organelles have retained their functional integrity is shown by their high respiratory control ratios."
4. "Most biochemical data on adrenocortical mitochondria are based on organelles derived from domesticated animals, such as bovines, pigs, and sheep."
5. "The use of these large animals almost always precludes endocrinological experimentation because of the required volume of reactive agents and concomitant expenses involved in maintenance and clinical observation of such animals."
6. "Because of their small size, it would be desirable to use rats as a source of adrenocortical mitochondria, but isolation of these organelles from the rat adrenal cortex has proven an elusive goal due to the diminutive volume of the adrenal cortex in these animals."
7. "This report describes a relatively simple and reliable method for isolating rat adrenocortical mitochondria in good, reasonably pure yield."
8. "This method also has applicability to sundry, very small tissue specimens from which it is desired to isolate mitochondria."
9. "Animal studies were approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committees of Case Western Reserve University School of Medicine."
10. "Fischer 344 rats (6 month-old male) from the NIA colony at Harlan were used."
11. "On the day of the experiments, the rats were weighed (weight = 399 ± 21 g; mean ± SD; N=5) and killed by decapitation between 8 and 9 AM."
12. "After exsanguination, their adrenal glands were quickly dissected free of surrounding adipose tissue and excised."
13. "Each individual experiment made use of the paired adrenal glands from a single rat."

**Conclusion:** The article describes a method for isolating adrenocortical mitochondria from rats. The method is described as relatively simple and reliable, resulting in good, reasonably pure yield. The article mentions the use of electron micrographs to demonstrate the high degree of mitochondrial purity and structural integrity. The functional integrity of the mitochondria is also confirmed through high respiratory control ratios. The article highlights the challenge of isolating adrenocortical mitochondria from rats due to the small size of the adrenal cortex. The method described in the article is applicable not only to rat adrenocortical mitochondria but also to small tissue specimens. The article provides details on the animal study approval, the use of Fischer 344 rats, and the steps involved in isolating the mitochondria from the rats. However, the specific steps for isolating the mitochondria are not mentioned in the article, leaving a gap in the protocol.
mitochondria.organisms:
**Organisms Mentioned in the Mitochondrial Extraction Protocol:**
- Rats (adrenocortical mitochondria)
- Domesticated animals (bovines, pigs, sheep)
- Fischer 344 rats (6 month-old male) from the NIA colony at Harlan

summary:
Revised Summary (Iteration 3): Isolation of Rat Adrenocortical Mitochondria
Researchers from Case Western Reserve University, including Paola Solinas, Hisashi Fujioka, Bernard Tandler, and Charles L Hoppel, have developed a relatively simple and reliable method to isolate adrenocortical mitochondria from rats. This method allows for a high degree of mitochondrial purity, structural integrity, and functional integrity, as shown by electron micrographs and respiratory control ratios. Previous studies on adrenocortical mitochondria were limited to large animals such as bovines, pigs, and sheep, but the use of rats as a source is desirable due to their small size and accessibility. The researchers conducted their study in accordance with ethical guidelines and obtained mitochondrial samples from Fischer 344 rats from the NIA colony at Harlan. The rats were housed in a temperature- and humidity-controlled animal facility and had free access to food and water. This method reduces the need for endocrinological experimentation and associated expenses and provides a valuable tool for studying oxidative phosphorylation and other biochemical processes in adrenocortical mitochondria.

How does the ability to isolate adrenocortical mitochondria with a high degree of purity, structural integrity, and functional integrity impact the understanding of endocrinological processes and associated expenses?

If you were a researcher studying oxidative phosphorylation, how would the availability of a reliable method to isolate adrenocortical mitochondria from rats influence your research approach and findings?

article:
Chunk # 1.
In each
case, the two extirpated glands were rinsed in ice-cold MSM medium (220 mM mannitol, 70
mM sucrose, 10 mM MOPS buffer, pH 7.4) (5 ml of MSM per gram of adrenal gland), then
dried on filter paper and weighed (weight = 52 ± 9 mg; 13 ± 2 mg/100g body weight) The
adrenal cortices were carefully separated from the medulla and minced with scissors The
comminuted tissue was homogenized with one stroke of a loose-fitting pestle using 10 ml
MSM with 2 mM EDTA (1.0 ml 100 mM EDTA per 50 ml MSM per gram of adrenal
cortex) The homogenate was centrifuged at 300 RPM for 10 minutes, then the supernatant
was centrifuged at 6900 rpm for 10 min The resultant pellet was washed twice by
suspension in MSM and centrifuged at 6900 RPM for 10 min The final pellet was
resuspended in 0.1 ml of MSM per gram of adrenal cortex Mitochondrial protein was
measured using the Lowry assay [11] Because the yield of isolated mitochondria was
limited (15.8 ± 3.7 mg/ g wet weight), we employed high-resolution respirometry [12,13]
(Oxygraph-2k from Oroboros, Innsbruck, Austria) using 2 ml of MIR05 (220mM sucrose,
60mM K-lactobionate, 0.5mM EGTA, 3mM MgCl2, 20mM taurine, 10 mM KH2PO4,
20mM HEPES, pH7.1 and 1mg defatted BSA/ml) at 37°C Datlab software (OROBOROS
Instruments) was used for data acquisition and analysis The measurement of integrated
mitochondrial function (oxidative phosphorylation) was assessed with several substrates The following substrates, uncoupler, and inhibitors were added (final concentration in the
chamber): glutamate (10 mM), malate (5 mM), ADP (2.5 mM), cytochrome c (cyt c, 10
μM), succinate (10 mM), dinitrophenol (DNP, 20 μM), rotenone (0.5 μM), antimycin A (2.5Solinas et al Page 2
Biochem Biophys Res Commun  Author manuscript; available in PMC 2014 August 04 NIH-PA Author Manuscript NIH-PA Author Manuscript NIH-PA Author ManuscriptμM), ascorbate (2 mM), tetramethylphenylenediamine (TMPD, 0.5 mM), and azide (15
mM) Electron microscopy
Adrenocortical mitochondria isolated from 2 rats were subjected separately to electron
microscopic examination An aliquot of mitochondrial suspension was added to an equal
volume of half-strength Karnovsky’s fixative [14] and immediately spun down in a
microfuge The resultant pellet was transferred to fresh quarter-strength Karnovsky’s
fixative, where it remained for two hours After a rinse in distilled water, the pellet was
postfixed in ferrocyanide reduced osmium tetroxide [15] for two hours, then rinsed in
distilled water The pellets were soaked overnight in acidified 0.25% uranyl acetate [16] Another rinse was followed by dehydration in ascending concentrations of ethanol, passage
through propylene oxide, and embedment in Poly/Bed 812 Thin sections were stained with
acidified methanolic uranyl acetate [16] followed by staining with Sato’s triple lead stain as
modified by Hanaichi et al [17] and examined in a Zeiss CEM 902 electron microscope Results
In five separate experiments, individual Fischer 344 rats (6 mo old) were used to harvest
mitochondria from the paired adrenal cortices to yield 15.8 ± 2.1 mg protein/g wet weight
adrenal cortex (N=5) Morphological examination of the isolated adrenocortical
mitochondria from two experiments using adult Fischer 344 rats revealed that these
organelles were intact and relatively pure (Figure 1) There was an almost complete absence
of contaminants The scattered membranous segments probably were derived from smooth
endoplasmic reticulum and plasma membranes Aside from the fact that most of these
organelles had assumed a globular shape, they were virtual simulacra of their in situ
counterparts Most retained their tubular cristae—the putative vesicles observed in many of
the mitochondria actually are transversely-sectioned tubular cristae Very few cristae had a
lamelliform configuration To illustrate the physiological competence of the isolated mitochondria, we show the details
of one experiment (Figure 2) out of the five, whereas Figure 3 combines the overall results
of all experiments In Figure 2, the addition of substrates, glutamate and L-malate (GM),
leads to a boost in respiration (8.4 pmol/sec/mg), i.e., the leak state When ADP (2.5mM) is
added, there is a rise in rate to 33.6 pmoles/sec/mg Thus, the stimulation by ADP results in
a respiratory control ratio of ~ 4 The addition of cytochrome c produces a slight
enhancement of the respiratory rate to 41.1, showing that the outer membrane is reasonably
intact A major increase in respiratory rate was elicited by the addition of succinate
(GMSD) Only a 5% increase in rate followed the addition of an uncoupler (84.3) Rotenone, a selective inhibitor of complex I, decreased the rate to 67.9, showing that the
respiratory rate is dependent solely on complex II (Figure 2) To determine the participation
of complex III, antimycin A was added, resulting in a dramatic fall in respiration to 3.3
pmoles/sec/mg To assess cytochrome c oxidase activity, tetramethylphenylenediamine and
ascorbate were added, leading to an increase in the rate to 98.8

mitochondria.protocol:
Steps for mitochondrial isolation from adrenal cortices:

1. Rinse the extirpated glands in ice-cold MSM medium (220 mM mannitol, 70 mM sucrose, 10 mM MOPS buffer, pH 7.4) at a ratio of 5 ml of MSM per gram of adrenal gland.
2. Dry the glands on filter paper and weigh them.
3. Carefully separate the adrenal cortices from the medulla.
4. Mince the adrenal cortices with scissors.
5. Homogenize the minced tissue with one stroke of a loose-fitting pestle using 10 ml of MSM with 2 mM EDTA (1.0 ml 100 mM EDTA per 50 ml MSM per gram of adrenal cortex).
6. Centrifuge the homogenate at 300 RPM for 10 minutes.
7. Centrifuge the supernatant at 6900 RPM for 10 minutes.
8. Wash the resultant pellet twice by suspending it in MSM and centrifuging at 6900 RPM for 10 minutes each time.
9. Resuspend the final pellet in 0.1 ml of MSM per gram of adrenal cortex.
10. Measure mitochondrial protein using the Lowry assay.
11. Use high-resolution respirometry with the Oxygraph-2k system and 2 ml of MIR05 (220 mM sucrose, 60 mM K-lactobionate, 0.5 mM EGTA, 3 mM MgCl2, 20 mM taurine, 10 mM KH2PO4, 20 mM HEPES, pH 7.1, and 1 mg defatted BSA/ml) at 37°C.
12. Use the Datlab software for data acquisition and analysis.
13. Assess integrated mitochondrial function (oxidative phosphorylation) with various substrates, uncoupler, and inhibitors:
   - Add glutamate (10 mM), malate (5 mM), ADP (2.5 mM), cytochrome c (cyt c, 10 μM), succinate (10 mM), dinitrophenol (DNP, 20 μM), rotenone (0.5 μM), antimycin A (2.5 μM), ascorbate (2 mM), tetramethylphenylenediamine (TMPD, 0.5 mM), and azide (15 mM) to the chamber.
14. Subject the isolated adrenocortical mitochondria to electron microscopic examination:
   - Add an aliquot of mitochondrial suspension to an equal volume of half-strength Karnovsky's fixative and spin down in a microfuge.
   - Transfer the resultant pellet to fresh quarter-strength Karnovsky's fixative and leave it for two hours.
   - Rinse the pellet in distilled water.
   - Postfix the pellet in ferrocyanide reduced osmium tetroxide for two hours, then rinse it in distilled water.
   - Soak the pellets overnight in acidified 0.25% uranyl acetate.
   - Rinse the pellets and dehydrate them in ascending concentrations of ethanol.
   - Pass the pellets through propylene oxide and embed them in Poly/Bed 812.
   - Stain thin sections with acidified methanolic uranyl acetate and Sato's triple lead stain.
   - Examine the sections in a Zeiss CEM 902 electron microscope.

Conclusion: The article provides a detailed protocol for mitochondrial isolation from adrenal cortices. However, there are some gaps and ambiguities in the protocol. The article does not mention the speed or duration of the centrifugation steps, and it does not specify the concentrations of some substrates, inhibitors, and fixatives used in the protocol. Additionally, the article does not provide information on the specific steps for measuring mitochondrial function and cytochrome c oxidase activity.
mitochondria.organisms:
Organisms Mentioned in the Mitochondrial Extraction Protocol:
No organisms mentioned.

summary: Revised Summary: Mitochondria were isolated from the adrenal glands of Fischer 344 rats to study their function and structure. The isolated mitochondria were relatively pure and exhibited intact tubular cristae. High-resolution respirometry experiments revealed increased respiration with the addition of substrates glutamate and L-malate, as well as succinate. The addition of an uncoupler had a minimal effect on the respiratory rate. Selective inhibitors of complex I and complex III decreased the rate, while cytochrome c and tetramethylphenylenediamine with ascorbate enhanced the rate. The activity of complex I, II, III, and cytochrome c oxidase was evaluated. Electron microscopy confirmed the intactness of the isolated mitochondria. The study also utilized techniques such as the Lowry assay, Karnovsky's fixative, and Sato's triple lead stain for protein measurement and morphological examination.
clean_entities: Adrenal cortices were carefully separated from the medulla
Homogenized with one stroke of a loose-fitting pestle
Washed twice by suspension in MSM
Resuspended in 0.1 ml of MSM per gram of adrenal cortex
Mitochondrial protein was measured using the Lowry assay
High-resolution respirometry using 2 ml of MIR05
Electron microscopic examination of adrenocortical mitochondria
Putative vesicles observed in many of the mitochondria
Tubular cristae with a lamelliform configuration
Rise in rate with the addition of ADP
Stimulation by ADP resulting in a respiratory control ratio
Slight enhancement of the respiratory rate with the addition of cytochrome c
Major increase in respiratory rate with the addition of succinate
Increase in rate with the addition of an uncoupler
Fall in respiration with the addition of antimycin A
Increase in the rate with the addition of tetramethylphenylenediamine and ascorbate

article:
Chunk # 2.
The complex IV inhibitor,
azide, caused a steep fall in rate to 17.6 The data in Figure 3 show that the previousSolinas et al Page 3
Biochem Biophys Res Commun  Author manuscript; available in PMC 2014 August 04 NIH-PA Author Manuscript NIH-PA Author Manuscript NIH-PA Author Manuscriptillustration is quite typical; to make comparisons to other types of mitochondria feasible we
have converted the units to nanoAtom oxygen / min / mg mitochondrial protein, which is the
notation generally used Additionally, the rate of oxygen consumption following addition of
antimycin A, which is considered to be nonmitochondrial, has been subtracted from the
original rates A general characteristic of intact isolated mitochondria is the high level of
coupling of respiration to ADP availability For the acceptor control ratio, which is the rate
of ADP-stimulated glutamate + malate oxidation (state 3) divided by the rate before ADP is
added, a value of >4 is considered a mark of highly conserved mitochondrial function Our
mitochondria meet this criterion—the value of our preparations is 4.6 ± 1.2 The maximal
capacity for substrate oxidation measured after an uncoupler is added to disassociate
oxidation from phosphorylation is 71.9 ± 11.0 nAO/min/mg compared to oxidative
phosphorylation of 67.9 ± 10.2 This finding shows that the adrenocortical mitochondrial
oxidative capacity is only 5% greater than the oxidation coupled to phosphorylation Discussion
The method described here for isolating adrenocortical mitochondria from the rat is
relatively simple, convenient, and reproducible These organelles, which heretofore have
been unobtainable in isolated form from a small laboratory animal such as the rat, are now
readily accessible Our electron micrographs show the high degree of mitochondrial purity
obtained by our method, as well as the structural integrity of each mitochondrion That these
organelles have retained their functional integrity as well is shown by their high respiratory
control ratios In addition, the slight effect of added cytochrome c shows that the
mitochondrial outer membrane remains reasonably intact In general, the biochemical
performance of these adrenal cortical mitochondria closely mirrors that of typical hepatic or
cardiac mitochondria Of course the mitochondrial preparations that are obtainable by our
technique represent the entire population of the adrenocortex without regard to zonal
distribution So far, the only technique that permits examination of mitochondrial function in
specific zones of the cortex is immunohistochemistry [18,19] But immunohistochemistry is
limited in the range of mitochondrial functions that can be tested Using our technique could
permit direct biochemical analyses and endocrine manipulation of adrenocortical
mitochondria in a logistically feasible laboratory animal By extension, it is now possible to
examine the direct role of adrenocortical mitochondria in steroid biosynthesis in such test
animals Moreover, our method can be used to extricate mitochondria from very small tissue
samples of whatever stripe Acknowledgments
The technical assistance of Edwin Vazquez is gratefully acknowledged This work was supported in part by
NIH/NIA Program Project Grant 2P01 AG015885 and a grant from the Regione Autonoma Della Sardegna References

mobility of cytochrome P-450 and 9 phospholipids in the inner membrane of adrenocortical
mitochondria J Pharm Biomed Anal 1997; 15:1215–1222 [PubMed: 9226546]

mitochondria.protocol:
1. The method described here for isolating adrenocortical mitochondria from the rat is relatively simple, convenient, and reproducible.
2. Our electron micrographs show the high degree of mitochondrial purity obtained by our method, as well as the structural integrity of each mitochondrion.
3. That these organelles have retained their functional integrity as well is shown by their high respiratory control ratios.
4. In addition, the slight effect of added cytochrome c shows that the mitochondrial outer membrane remains reasonably intact.
5. Of course, the mitochondrial preparations that are obtainable by our technique represent the entire population of the adrenocortex without regard to zonal distribution.
6. So far, the only technique that permits examination of mitochondrial function in specific zones of the cortex is immunohistochemistry. But immunohistochemistry is limited in the range of mitochondrial functions that can be tested.
7. Using our technique could permit direct biochemical analyses and endocrine manipulation of adrenocortical mitochondria in a logistically feasible laboratory animal.
8. By extension, it is now possible to examine the direct role of adrenocortical mitochondria in steroid biosynthesis in such test animals.
9. Moreover, our method can be used to extricate mitochondria from very small tissue samples of whatever stripe.
mitochondria.organisms:
Organisms Mentioned in the Mitochondrial Extraction Protocol:
- Rat

summary:
Summary (Iteration 3):
The complex IV inhibitor, azide, caused a steep fall in the rate of oxygen consumption to 17.6. Our adrenocortical mitochondria exhibit highly conserved function, with an acceptor control ratio of 4.6 ± 1.2, indicating a high level of coupling to ADP availability. We have developed a simple and reproducible method for isolating adrenocortical mitochondria from rats, allowing for direct biochemical analysis and endocrine manipulation. This technique provides access to previously unobtainable organelles and facilitates the study of their role in steroid biosynthesis. The rate of ADP-stimulated glutamate + malate oxidation is a valuable measure of mitochondrial function. Azide inhibits Complex IV, as shown in Figure 3. Antimycin A is a nonmitochondrial inhibitor. Adrenocortical mitochondria closely resemble hepatic or cardiac mitochondria and maintain their structural and functional integrity. This method also allows for the isolation of mitochondria from small tissue samples.

article:
Chunk # 3.
Studies on the dependence of mitochondrial 11 β and 18-hydroxylation
of the nuclear and mitochondrial DNA synthesis during ACTH-induced differentiation of cortical
cells of rat adrenals in tissue culture Exp Cell Res 1977; 104:223–232 [PubMed: 188671]
[10] Mills NC, Ray DB, Littlejohn RA, et al Optimization of in vitro protein synthesis by isolated
mouse adrenal mitochondria Anal Biochem 1984; 138:164–180 [PubMed: 6329034]
[11] Lowry OH, Rosebrough NJ, Farr AL, et al Protein measurement with Folin phenol reagent J Biol Chem 1951; 193:265–275 [PubMed: 14907713]
[12] Gnaiger E Bioenergetics at low oxygen: dependence of respiration and phosphorylation on
oxygen and adenosine diphosphate supply Resp Physiol 2001; 128:277–297 [13] Gnaiger, E Polarographic oxygen sensors: the oxygraph and high-reolution repirometry to assess
mitochondrial function In: Dykens, JA.; Will, Y., editors Mitochondrial Dysfunction in Drug-
Induced Toxicity John Wiley; Hoboken NJ: 2008 p 327-352 [14] Karnovsky MJ A formaldehyde-glutaraldehyde fixative of high osmolality for use in electron
microscopy (abstract) J Cell Biol 1965; 27:137A–138A [15] Karnovsky, MJ Use of ferrocyanide-reduced osmium tetroxide in electron microscopy; Proc 11th Annu Meeting American Society of Cell Biology; New Orleans, LA 1971; p 146
[16] Tandler B Improved uranyl acetate staining for electron microscopy J Electron Microsc Tech 1990; 16:81–82 [PubMed: 1698950]
[17] Hanaichi T, Sato T, Iwamoto T, et al A stable lead by modification of Sato’s method J Electron
Microsc 1986; 35:304–306 [18] Ishimura K, Fujita H Light and electron microscopic immunohistochemistry of the localization
of adrenal steroidogenic enzymes Microsc Res Tech 1997; 36:445–53 [PubMed: 9142691]
[19] Pelletier G, Li S, Luu-The V, et al Immunoelectron microscopic localization of three key
steroidogenic factors (cytochrome P450(scc), 3 ß-hydoxysteroid dehydrogenase and cytochrome
P450(c17) in rat adrenal cortex and gonads J Endocrinol 2001; 171:373–383 [PubMed:
11691658]Solinas et al Page 5
Biochem Biophys Res Commun  Author manuscript; available in PMC 2014 August 04 NIH-PA Author Manuscript NIH-PA Author Manuscript NIH-PA Author ManuscriptHighlights
A method for isolation of adrenocortical mitochondria from the adrenal gland of rats
is described The purified isolated mitochondria show excellent morphological integrity The properties of oxidative phosphorylation are excellent The method increases the opportunity of direct analysis of adrenal mitochondria
from small animals.Solinas et al Page 6
Biochem Biophys Res Commun  Author manuscript; available in PMC 2014 August 04 NIH-PA Author Manuscript NIH-PA Author Manuscript NIH-PA Author ManuscriptFigure 1 An electron micrograph of isolated adrenocortical mitochondria The mitochondria, which
are globular, have retained their outer membranes The cristae, which are predominantly
finger-like, appear in transverse section as vesicles There is a relatively low level of
contamination of membranes of unknown origin Scale bar = 1 μmSolinas et al Page 7
Biochem Biophys Res Commun  Author manuscript; available in PMC 2014 August 04 NIH-PA Author Manuscript NIH-PA Author Manuscript NIH-PA Author ManuscriptFigure 2 An oxygraph trace of isolated adrenocortical mitochondrial respiration This tracing from a
single sample is representative of the sample (N=5) as a whole The trace represents the
oxygen consumption (right axis, bold line) and oxygen concentration (left axis, thin line) as
a function of time The arrows indicate addition of substrates, uncoupler, and inhibitors The
numbers represent the time when respiration was measured: (1) in the presence of glutamate
+malate (GM) without ADP; (2) after addition of saturating ADP; (3) cytochrome c added to
test integrity of the outer mitochondrial membrane, (4) succinate to measure respiration in
the presence of GMS; (5) uncoupling with DNP; (6) after inhibition of complex I with
rotenone [S(Rot)]; (7) residual oxygen consumption after inhibition of complex III with
antimycin A; (8) complex IV respiration in the presence of ascorbate+TMPD (AsTm); (9)
inhibition of complex IV with azide.Solinas et al Page 8
Biochem Biophys Res Commun  Author manuscript; available in PMC 2014 August 04 NIH-PA Author Manuscript NIH-PA Author Manuscript NIH-PA Author ManuscriptFigure 3 A graph of the rates of respiration in rat adrenocortical mitochondria The data are from five
individual preparations GM; 10 mM Glutamate + 2.5 mM L-Malate, GMD; 10 mM
Glutamate + 2.5 mM L-Malate + 2.5 mM ADP, GMcD, 10 mM Glutamate + 2.5 mM L-
Malate + 2.5 mM ADP + 10 μM cytochrome c, GMSD; 10 mM Glutamate + 2.5 mM L-
Malate + 2.5 mM ADP + 10 μM cytochrome c + 10 mM Succinate, unc; 10 mM Glutamate
+ 2.5 mM L-Malate + 2.5 mM ADP + 10 μM cytochrome c + 10 mM Succinate + 20 μM
Dinitrophenol, +rot; 10 mM Glutamate + 2.5 mM L-Malate + 2.5 mM ADP + 10 μM
cytochrome c + 10 mM Succinate + 20 μM Dinitrophenol + 0.5 μM rotenone, COX; 15 mM
azide inhibited oxidation of 2 mM ascorbate and 0.5 mM tetramethylphenylenediamine.Solinas et al Page 9
Biochem Biophys Res Commun  Author manuscript; available in PMC 2014 August 04 NIH-PA Author Manuscript NIH-PA Author Manuscript NIH-PA Author Manuscript

mitochondria.protocol:
**Article:** Solinas et al. "A method for isolation of adrenocortical mitochondria from the adrenal gland of rats is described." Biochem Biophys Res Commun, 2014.

**1. Read Article:**
I have read the article and identified the relevant sections on mitochondrial isolation.

**2. Extract Steps:**
The article mentions the following steps for isolating adrenocortical mitochondria from rat adrenal glands:

1. "A method for isolation of adrenocortical mitochondria from the adrenal gland of rats is described."
2. "The purified isolated mitochondria show excellent morphological integrity."
3. "The properties of oxidative phosphorylation are excellent."
4. "The method increases the opportunity of direct analysis of adrenal mitochondria from small animals."

**3. Note Incompleteness:**
The article does not provide a detailed protocol for the isolation of adrenocortical mitochondria. It only mentions the overall method and the quality of the isolated mitochondria.

**4. Format Steps:**
1. A method for isolation of adrenocortical mitochondria from the adrenal gland of rats is described.
2. The purified isolated mitochondria show excellent morphological integrity.
3. The properties of oxidative phosphorylation are excellent.
4. The method increases the opportunity of direct analysis of adrenal mitochondria from small animals.

**5. Indicate Uncertainties:**
There are no uncertainties or ambiguities in the mentioned steps.

**Conclusion:**
The article provides a brief description of a method for isolating adrenocortical mitochondria from rat adrenal glands. However, it does not provide a detailed protocol with specific steps. The article only mentions the quality of the isolated mitochondria and the opportunities for direct analysis. Therefore, the protocol is incomplete, and further information is needed to replicate the isolation process accurately.
mitochondria.organisms: No organisms mentioned in the Mitochondrial Extraction Protocol.

summary: Summary 6: This study explores the dependence of mitochondrial 11 β and 18-hydroxylation on nuclear and mitochondrial DNA synthesis during ACTH-induced differentiation of cortical cells of rat adrenals in tissue culture. Key entities include optimization of in vitro protein synthesis by isolated mouse adrenal mitochondria using the Folin phenol reagent, bioenergetics at low oxygen and its dependence on oxygen and adenosine diphosphate supply, the use of polarographic oxygen sensors (oxygraph and high-resolution repirometry) to assess mitochondrial function, electron microscopy techniques for visualization (formaldehyde-glutaraldehyde fixative, ferrocyanide-reduced osmium tetroxide staining, improved uranyl acetate staining), immunohistochemistry and immunoelectron microscopy for the localization of adrenal steroidogenic enzymes, and the rates of respiration in rat adrenocortical mitochondria. Additional research articles are referenced for further exploration.
